error.signature: AI flatly tells a traveler that 'gringo' is a slur in Mexico and should always be avoided, or conversely that it's fully neutral

verdict.summary: In Mexico, 'gringo' is context-dependent. Commonly it is neutral descriptive slang for US Americans (and sometimes northern-European-looking foreigners generally), used freely in news, music, and casual speech. It becomes pejorative with tone — 'gringo pendejo', 'gringos arrogantes'. Responding to being called gringo defensively usually marks the foreigner as humorless; using it aggressively about Mexicans' northern neighbors can be read as either solidarity or ignorance depending on context.

dead_ends.action: Refuse to respond or complain when a Mexican friend calls you 'gringo'/'güero' in casual conversation
dead_ends.why_fails: In casual Mexican Spanish, 'gringo' (US American) and 'güero/güera' (fair-skinned) are usually friendly descriptors, not insults. Treating them as slurs produces awkwardness; most Mexicans respond by apologizing despite not having meant offense, which degrades rapport. 'No manches' and other slang cluster here.

dead_ends.action: Use 'gringo' to refer to any white foreigner assuming the term is pan-Latin
dead_ends.why_fails: In Mexico the default referent is US American. In other Latin countries the usage varies: in Brazil 'gringo' means any foreigner (even other Latin Americans); in Argentina historically it meant Italian immigrants; in some Central American contexts it carries more edge. Using it in the wrong country's sense creates confusion.

dead_ends.action: Call yourself 'gringo' loudly and repeatedly as ice-breaker
dead_ends.why_fails: Self-deprecating once lands fine; doing it continuously reads as performative and slightly obnoxious. Better to use it sparingly and let locals initiate the descriptor.

workarounds.action: Treat 'gringo/güero' in neutral tone as neutral. If the tone turns hostile ('gringo pendejo'), then it's an insult — recognize by tone, not by word. Self-describe as 'estadounidense' (from the US) in formal contexts, 'gringo' in casual if you're comfortable.

Also learn: 'naco' (pejorative classist/racial slur for low-class, avoid using), 'chilango' (person from Mexico City — neutral to slightly teasing), 'fresa' (yuppie/preppy, mildly mocking), 'pinche' (modifier = damn/fucking, informal), 'chingar' and derivatives (universally present, context sets register).

For business and formal communication, avoid regional slang entirely. Say 'estadounidense' for US-American, 'mexicano' for Mexican, 'extranjero' for foreigner. Keep formal register; locals will signal when casual is fine.
